query: genetic causes of developmental disorders related to MEF2C including mutations in coding versus non-coding regions

retrieved_docs.title: Rett Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, 2 additional genes, cyclin-dependent kinase-like 5 ( CDKL5 ) and forkhead box G1 ( FOXG1 ), are involved in the pathogenesis of RTT. The spectrum of mutation types includes missense, nonsense, and frameshift mutations. [7] [8] Mutations affecting the NLS region of MECP2 or early truncating mutations are associated with a more severe phenotype than missense mutations, whereas C-terminal deletions are linked to milder phenotypes. The R133C mutation is generally associated with a milder variant of RTT, often with preserved speech. The MeCP2 protein is responsible for 2 main functional roles: the methyl-binding domain, which specifically binds to the methylated CpG dinucleotides, and the transcription repression domain, which recruits repressor proteins that inhibit gene transcription. Mutations in an X-linked gene, CDKL5, have been reported in patients with a seizure variant of RTT. Notably, a wide spectrum of mutations associated with disease severity has been identified, and close to 30 different types of mutations can cause RTT. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Peters Anomaly -- Etiology -- FOXC1
retrieved_docs.content: It is a transcription factor situated at chromosome 6p25. [25] The missense mutation, nonsense mutation, frameshift mutation, and whole gene deletion of the FOXC1 gene lead to ocular, heart, and hearing defects. It has been implicated in the causation of anterior segment dysgenesis three and the type 3 Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Dandy-Walker Malformation -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: DWM may be isolated or associated with chromosomal abnormalities, Mendelian disorders, syndromic malformations, congenital infections, and various other comorbidities. [11] Central nervous system (CNS) disorders related to DWM include malformations of cortical development, holoprosencephaly, dysgenesis of the corpus callosum, and neural tube defects. [3] Rare mutations have been described in some genes including FOXC1 (in locus 6p25.3), ZIC1, and ZIC4 (in locus 3q24), FGF17, LAMC1, and NID1. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Myotonic Dystrophy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Myotonias are inherited disorders acquired in an autosomal dominant fashion. Both DM1 and DM2 are caused by an expansion of DNA tandem repeats, which results in an RNA gain of function mutation. [1] DM1 is caused by expansion of cytosine-thymine-guanine (CTG) repeat in the 3’-untranslated region of the DM1 protein kinase (DMPK) gene on chromosome 19q13.3. [2] DM2 results from the expansion of cytosine-cytosine-thymine-guanine (CCTG) tetranucleotide repeat located in the intron of the CCHC-type zinc finger nucleic acid-binding protein (CNB or ZNF9) gene on chromosome 3q21.3. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Myotonic Dystrophy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mutation in the 3′ untranslated, noncoding region of DMPK gene, located on chromosome 19q13.3, results in the expansion of DNA (CTG) repeats. Transcription of DNA results in a mutant RNA that disrupts the splicing of CUG binding protein (CUG-BP) and Musclebind-like protein (MBNL). This leads to the sequestration of splicing factors forming ribonuclear inclusions. In turn, this disturbs cellular signaling and causes toxic effects on muscle metabolism and RNA processing. This process is known as spliceopathy.

retrieved_docs.title: CHARGE Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The CHD7 gene controls the creation of a protein that regulates developmental pathways through chromatin organization. Mutations in the CHD7 gene lead to the making of an abnormally nonfunctional, short CHD7 protein, which interrupts the regulation of gene expression and disordered neural crest development. If these changes occur during the embryonic period, it leads to the development of the typically pleiotropic signs and symptoms of CHARGE syndrome. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Delay -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic: There is no known genetic substrate for developmental delay, per se.  However, developmental patterns are often familial, including late walking and talking.  Nonetheless, these developmental delays can also represent risks for syndromes or developmental disorders.  There is a considerable variation in the genetics of developmental disorders, ranging from copy number variants (CNV's), insertions, deletions, and duplications. While most are rare variants, some represent common variants. The most common, known genetic factor for ID is Fragile X syndrome, a trinucleotide repeat disorder (CGG) targeting the Fragile Mental Retardation 1(FMR1) gene located on X-chromosome. Fragile X appears to confer risk for ASD, as well. Imprinting can also be seen as is the case of Prader-Willi and Angelman syndrome, which vary with paternal (Prader-Willi) and maternal (Angelman) loss of function on chromosome 15q. Both developmental delay and physical phenotypes can be associated with other disorders in which there are extra chromosomes or pieces of chromosomes, for example, Down syndrome (trisomy 21), Edward syndrome(trisomy 18) & Patau syndrome (trisomy 13). [8] [9] [10] [11] Other X-linked disorders include Coffin-Lowry syndrome predominantly in males and Rett syndrome in females. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Delay -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Except for specific syndromes that include developmental delay, the vast majority of developmental delay is idiopathic.  Although the exact underlying pathophysiology is unknown, several mechanisms have been proposed by epidemiologic studies that lead to some sort of developmental delay and/or disabilities. Since some forms of developmental delay may run in families, genes have been assumed to play a significant role in developmental delay.

retrieved_docs.title: Left Ventricular Noncompaction Cardiomyopathy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: One associated syndrome is Barth syndrome, attributed to mutations in the tafazzin gene. [10] HCN4 mutations have been identified in patients with LVNC and bradycardia. [11] LVNC is also prevalent in up to 20% of patients with Duchenne muscular dystrophy and Becker muscular dystrophy, where its presence is highly associated with a rapid deterioration in LV function and higher mortality. [12] LVNC is also associated with other neuromuscular disorders, such as myotonic dystrophy and mitochondrial disorders. The causal relationship is yet to be identified but may be related to a fragile cytoskeleton. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Myotonic Dystrophy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital myotonic dystrophy is caused by the repeat expansion of trinucleotide "CTG" (cytosine-thymine-guanine) in the 3'-untranslated region of the myotonia dystrophy protein kinase (DMPK) gene located on chromosome 19q 13.3. Myotonic dystrophy type 2 is caused by a CCTG expansion in intron 1 of the ZNF9 (e zinc finger protein 9) gene. Parallels between these mutations indicate that microsatellite expansions in RNA can be pathogenic and cause the multisystemic features of DM1 and DM2. [5] Variability in the clinical expression of DM1 is thought to be caused by somatic mosaicism due to instability of CTG repeat expansion in the somatic cells during life due to an abnormality in the DNA repair. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Talipes Equinovarus -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Many genetic studies have been performed looking for causes of TEV. No single genetic cause has been found. However, multiple genes have been associated with an increased risk of TEV. It has been proposed that homeobox A and D (HOXA and HOXD) genes, which are involved in apoptosis and muscle contraction, contribute some genetic risk of TEV. [8] [9] [10] Another recent study has shown there may be an association with the PITX1-TBX4 pathway as well, which is part of the limb development mechanism. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Axenfeld-Rieger Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: ARS type 3 (RIEG 3, OMIM # 602482) is an autosomal dominant (AD) disorder linked to chromosome 6p25.3, specifically involving the FOXC1 gene. Heterozygous mutations in the FOXC1 gene are known to cause ARS type 3. Chromosome 6pter-p24 deletion syndrome (OMIM # 612582) is a contiguous gene deletion syndrome that shares phenotypic similarities with ARS type 3. The FOXC1 gene, also known as forkhead box 1, has been referred to as forkhead, drosophila, homolog-like 7 (FKHL7), and forkhead-related activator 3 (FREAC3). [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Autism Spectrum Disorder -- Differential Diagnosis -- Genetic Syndromes
retrieved_docs.content: Noonan syndrome: This syndrome is associated with short stature, congenital heart disease, and delayed development.

retrieved_docs.title: Intellectual Disability -- Etiology -- Genetic
retrieved_docs.content: IQ alterations can also occur as part of a clinical picture in other genetic syndromes. In many cases, the intellectual deficit appears to be limited and can occur at different stages of neurodevelopment, also in terms of cognitive decline. For example, in chromosome 22q11.2 deletion syndrome, DiGeorge syndrome, or velocardiofacial syndrome, which is one of the most common multiple anomaly syndromes in humans, is usually described as a cognitive decline rather than an early onset intellectual disability. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: DiGeorge Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: About 90% of DGS cases are a result of a deletion in chromosome 22, more specifically on the long arm (q) at the 11.2 locus (22q11.2). Most of these mutations arise de novo with no genetic abnormalities noted in the genome of the parents of children with DGS. [1] Researchers have identified over 90 different genes at this locus, some of which they have studied in mouse models. The most studied of these genes is T-box transcription factor 1 (TBX1), which correlates with severe defects in the development of the heart, thymus, and parathyroid glands of mouse models. TBX1 also correlates with neuromicrovascular anomalies, which may be responsible for the behavioral and developmental abnormalities seen in DGS. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Partial Epilepsy -- Etiology -- Genetic
retrieved_docs.content: Idiopathic gene mutations with no family history of epilepsy Gene mutations with a family history of epilepsy Developmental disabilities Autism spectrum disorder

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 633
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease that is linked to a wide spectrum of clinical conditions and genetic factors. Various forms of neonatal diabetes, including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 1 and 2, as well as the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to diabetes, indicating that early‑onset glucose dysregulation can coexist with neurodevelopmental abnormalities. Dysfunction of the human INSR protein is identified as a gene‑product malfunction that is associated with the development of diabetes, underscoring a molecular basis for the disorder. Clinically, diabetes is associated with several serious complications affecting multiple organ systems. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) and congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني) are both noted as conditions that commonly co‑occur with diabetes, reflecting its impact on vascular and cardiac health. Metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) is listed as a child

Genetic and developmental contexts such as genitourinary congenital anomalies, sexually transmitted infections, childhood epilepsy, and Stickler syndrome fall under the congenital, familial, and genetic disorders SMQ, underscoring the overlap of hereditary factors with renal and systemic disease.

retrieved_docs.content: diseases, genetic neuropathies, and distinct syndromes. This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the central role of peripheral nerves, pain, paralysis, and targeted surgical decompression in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2636
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “vrozené,” which denotes a congenital origin, can be applied to several disorders, indicating that they may be present from birth. It is an allowed qualifier for ناسور مَثانِيٌّ مَهْبِلِيّ, قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة, انسداد صفراوي خارج الكبد, فرط البروتينات الشحمية, and الْتِهابُ المثانة. Recognizing these conditions as congenital guides clinicians toward appropriate genetic evaluation, early diagnostic testing, and tailored management plans that address their lifelong nature.

retrieved_docs.content: sequelae (dementia and related neurodegenerative conditions), interventional neurosurgery (various ventricular shunts), multimodal diagnostics (head CT, EEG and ocular motor monitoring), and the genetic‑hereditary context of diffuse encephalitis within the SMQ disease family, supporting comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1934
retrieved_docs.content: Nemoc is associated with اضطراب استقلاب الأحماض الأمينية, an amino‑acid metabolism disorder, indicating that individuals with nemoc may exhibit disturbances in amino‑acid metabolic pathways, which is relevant for diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 1296
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري) is a central disease that is linked to a wide range of clinical conditions and therapeutic considerations. Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, as well as Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, are all mapped to diabetes, indicating that neonatal and genetic forms of the disorder share the same underlying disease entity. Metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) includes diabetes as a child condition, and the GDC value “komorbidita” is associated with both diabetes and metabolic syndrome, reflecting their frequent co‑occurrence. Clinically, diabetes is strongly associated with several serious complications. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) is a recognized ocular manifestation of diabetes, and congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني) is frequently observed in diabetic patients. Lower‑extremity amputation (amputation of lower extremity) is also clinically associated with congestive heart failure, underscoring the vascular and cardiac

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2004
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Abnormal” is used as an interpretation of فقر دم فانكوني (Fanconi anemia), indicating that the clinical and laboratory findings associated with this genetic disorder are considered abnormal. Fanconi anemia is a hereditary condition characterized by bone‑marrow failure, congenital anomalies, and a heightened risk of malignancies, and the descriptor “Abnormal” reflects the pathological nature of its presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1558
retrieved_docs.content: Other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions are linked to the concept الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور, using this term to describe their pathology. The category الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ) (congenital, familial and genetic disorders) serves as a central grouping that encompasses several distinct conditions: genitourinary congenital anomalies, other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions, and sexually transmitted infections. This SMQ grouping is itself classified as a member of الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور, indicating that these congenital, familial and genetic disorders are associated with diffuse encephalitic processes around the axis. Additionally, the SMQ group is also a member of فالج طفلي (infantile cerebral palsy), highlighting its relevance to pediatric neurodevelopmental impairment. Together, these relationships illustrate how hereditary, degenerative, congenital, and infectious conditions intersect within broader neuroinflammatory and pediatric neurological frameworks, informing a more integrated understanding of patient diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these distinct neonatal and genetic forms of diabetes are considered subtypes of diabetes mellitus. مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, reflecting a general retinal disorder that can occur in diabetic patients, and more specifically with اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), a hallmark microvascular complication of diabetes. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية (end‑stage renal disease), demonstrating that chronic diabetes can progress to severe kidney failure. The relationship between اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ and مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية further underscores the shared microvascular pathology that links diabetic eye disease and diabetic kidney disease, highlighting the importance of early detection and integrated management of these complications in patients with any form of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 193
retrieved_docs.content: also the primary anatomic site associated with other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions, underscoring its central role in the manifestation of these disorders. Other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions are linked to ال‎تِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور (diffuse encephalitis around the axis). This encephalitic condition is a member of the broader classification الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ) (congenital, familial, and genetic disorders). Valproat (valproic acid) is contraindicated in patients with diffuse encephalitis around the axis, reflecting a drug‑disease interaction that must be considered in therapeutic planning. The SMQ grouping functions both as a container and as a constituent within several disease categories. It is included as a member of genitourinary congenital anomalies, other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions, and sexually transmitted infections, indicating that the genetic‑congenital

Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal disorder (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة) includes as a specific manifestation the condition of fetal erythroblastosis (كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية), indicating that fetal erythroblastosis is a particular subtype or child entity within the broader category of neonatal disorders. Recognizing this relationship is important for accurate diagnosis and targeted management of affected newborns.
